check: 51 states present in all tables
question: Do we have all 51 states in every table?

check: No duplicate keys (vintage: state x month; current: state x adj x month)
question: Is (state, adjustment, month) a unique key?

check: No nulls in key fields
question: Are key fields complete (no nulls)?

check: All payroll levels strictly positive and finite
question: Is every payroll level strictly positive and finite?
note: employment is a large positive stock (thousands) - no zeros/negatives expected, unlike claims

check: SA series length consistent across states
question: Are the series lengths consistent across states?
note: all SA state series begin 1990-01 by construction

check: All obs_date are month-starts (monthly reference convention)
question: Are all observation dates month-starts?

check: No missing months per state (SA)
question: Are there missing months in any state?

check: Panel edges aligned across states
question: Do all states cover the same window?
note: balanced monthly panel 1990-01 onward

check: ALFRED vintage boundary identified
question: How far back can point-in-time be reconstructed?
note: pre-boundary history exists only as current (fully-revised) values: usable for trend/seasonality estimation, NOT for point-in-time backtesting

check: National PAYEMS vintages land on the jobs-report day (Friday)
question: Does the vintage calendar match the jobs-report schedule?
note: the national Employment Situation is released the first Friday after the reference month

check: State publication lag ~50 days (joint state emp/unemp report)
question: Is the state publication lag ~50 days?
note: ~7 weeks after the reference month, i.e. ~3 weeks after the national jobs report

check: State NFP published after the national jobs report
question: Do state releases follow the national jobs report?
metric: 100.0% of state months released after national; median gap +14d
note: state detail follows the national aggregate by ~3 weeks - a state feature is never available before the national print for the same month

check: Publication-lag regime stable over time
question: Is the publication-lag regime stable over time?
note: a stable lag lets a backtest assume a single availability rule (unlike claims, which had a 2020 capture-regime switch)

check: Release dates weakly increasing in obs order (per state)
question: Do months become available in order?
note: the PIT builder uses the cummax release frontier, so small inversions are harmless

check: PIT panel look-ahead guard (nfp_latest_obs released <= as_of_date)
question: Does the PIT panel contain any look-ahead?

check: Endpoint revision rate & magnitude (first release vs current)
question: How often/how much does a first print get revised?
metric: 99.2% of months revised; median |rev| 0.55%, p90 1.63%, max 7.5%
threshold: informational - NFP is heavily revised by design
note: the OPPOSITE of claims (~0% revised): current values embed benchmark corrections unknown in real time, so backtests MUST use first-release / vintage data

check: Systematic bias in first prints
question: Is there a systematic bias in first prints?
metric: mean revision +0.052% (first prints understate on average)
note: a persistent sign means first prints are predictably off - a modellable nowcast signal, and a reason never to mix first-release features with current-data targets

check: Revisions cluster at the annual benchmark (Feb/Mar release months)
question: Do revisions cluster at the annual benchmark?
metric: 95% of largest revisions land in Jan-Mar; modal release month = 3 (1249 of 1308 obs across CA/TX/NY)
threshold: >= 60% of biggest revisions in the winter benchmark window
note: confirms the annual QCEW re-anchoring drives the big revisions; a backtest must apply the benchmark on its release date, not spread it backward into the feature history

check: National PAYEMS revision benchmark quantified
question: How large is the national benchmark (calibration)?
note: the national series is revised on the same schedule; its magnitude anchors what to expect state-by-state

check: Extreme monthly moves classified (|robust z| > 6 on dlog)
question: Which extreme moves are real (COVID) vs unexplained?
note: the 2020-03/04 collapse and rebound dominate; classify and regime-flag rather than winsorize away

check: No long stale-value runs (>= 3 identical months)
question: Are there stale/frozen stretches?
note: SA employment levels almost never repeat exactly; a run would signal a data hold

check: Few extreme monthly moves outside COVID
question: Are there level breaks beyond COVID?
note: monthly payroll growth is normally < 1%; large non-COVID moves flag small states or data events

check: Sum(51 states) reconciles with national PAYEMS
question: Do the 51 states sum to national PAYEMS?
note: state CES sums almost exactly to national (both SA total nonfarm) - a much tighter identity than claims, whose national excludes PR/VI

check: Cross-state co-movement (mean pairwise corr of YoY growth)
question: Does every state co-move with the national cycle?
note: payroll cycles are highly synchronised across states; a decorrelated state is a data suspect

check: Seasonal adjustment removed the seasonality (SA vs NSA month-dummy R2)
question: Did SA actually remove the seasonality?
metric: month-of-year R2 of MoM growth: SA 0.01 vs NSA 0.88
note: SA data should carry little month-of-year structure; strong residual seasonality would mean the published adjustment is inadequate for the modelling frequency

check: Transform recommendation (levels I(1) trending; growth stationary)
question: Are levels trending and growth stationary?
metric: California: lvl p=0.90, growth p=0.000; Texas: lvl p=0.78, growth p=0.000; New York: lvl p=0.74, growth p=0.000; Florida: lvl p=0.84, growth p=0.000; Ohio: lvl p=0.32, growth p=0.000
threshold: MoM growth stationary (p<0.05) for all sample states
note: model payroll GROWTH (MoM or YoY log-diff), never levels; levels are trending and non-stationary - the key contrast with cyclical claims/unemployment

check: Payroll growth is persistent (AR1 of MoM growth, pre-COVID)
question: Is payroll growth persistent?
note: positive persistence supports momentum features; near-zero would imply monthly growth is noise

check: PIT features indexed by information time (monthly as-of grid)
question: Are features indexed by information time?
metric: monthly as-of grid: True; median data-staleness 2 months
note: at as-of month T the latest available reference month is ~T-2 (the publication lag)

check: Orientation: payroll growth moves opposite to unemployment (Okun sign)
question: Does payroll move opposite to unemployment (Okun)?
metric: California: corr -0.94; Texas: corr -0.90; New York: corr -0.94; Florida: corr -0.90; Ohio: corr -0.90
note: validates sign/alignment wiring: more jobs <-> lower unemployment. NOT the analysis itself

check: Monthly alignment convention documented
question: How to align with monthly indicators?
metric: both NFP and LAUS reference the week/pay-period incl. the 12th
note: NFP (payroll) and LAUS (household) share the 12th-of-month reference, so monthly joins align directly - but respect the ~3-week-longer NFP-state publication lag for real-time work

check: PIT coverage complete after ramp-in
question: From which date is the PIT panel complete?
note: usable backtest window starts 2008-08-01

check: Universe stability (balanced panel)
question: Is the universe stable?

check: Current-vs-vintage contamination if PIT were ignored
question: How badly would current-instead-of-vintage contaminate a backtest?
metric: median |gap| 0.59%, p90 1.68%, max 7.5% between current and real-time latest value
note: a backtest using current data would train on values the modeller could NOT have seen; unlike claims (~0% gap), NFP demands vintage features. This is the central backtesting finding.

check: Manifest written (hashes, params, ranges)
question: Can the dataset be reproduced and audited?
note: ALFRED queries with pinned realtime params are reproducible; re-fetch and diff hashes to audit
